nativeRange.habitat: Sandy, muddy, and detrital substrates on shallow tropical reefs and lagoons, where the shrimp excavates and maintains a burrow used as a shared shelter with a partner goby. The burrow is constantly rebuilt as sand collapses, and the shrimp carries debris in and out using its enlarged cheliped.
nativeRange.biotope: Sandy reef flats and lagoonal slopes adjacent to coral and rubble structure

decorPreferences: fine sand or sand-and-rubble bed at least 5 cm deep for burrow construction
live rock with a stable foundation set directly on the bottom glass so excavated burrows do not collapse rockwork
shaded crevices and low to moderate flow over the substrate
a compatible partner goby (Cryptocentrus, Amblyeleotris, or Stonogobiops) sharing the burrow
dim or shaded lighting in the burrow area

diet.notes: Generally considered easy to feed. In the wild it scavenges small invertebrates, detritus, carrion, and the occasional bit of macroalgae; in captivity it readily takes mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty seafood, sinking pellets, and bottom-feeder tablets. A partner goby will typically share food carried into the burrow, and the shrimp also harvests scraps the goby drops while feeding.

goodWith: partner gobies (Cryptocentrus cinctus, Amblyeleotris guttata, Stonogobiops yasha, Amblyeleotris japonica)
small peaceful reef fish (firefish, cardinalfish, small wrasses)
other peaceful shrimp at a distance (one pistol shrimp per tank unless very large)
avoidWith: other pistol or mantis shrimp in the same burrow zone (territorial disputes)
predatory fish that hunt invertebrates (large hawkfish, lionfish, triggers, large wrasses, pufferfish)
very small ornamental shrimp that could be mistaken for prey
loose or unsupported rockwork that could collapse over excavated burrows

summary: Famous burrow-digging snapping shrimp (~4-7 cm) that pairs with watchman gobies in a classic mutualistic symbiosis. Generally considered reef-safe and beginner-friendly, with a tan, rust-striped body and an enlarged claw capable of a cavitation-bubble snap.
careNotes: Tiger Pistol Shrimp are generally considered one of the most rewarding beginner saltwater invertebrates because of their dramatic burrowing behaviour and well-known symbiosis with shrimpgobies of the genera Cryptocentrus, Amblyeleotris, and Stonogobiops. The shrimp has poor eyesight and relies on the goby as a sentry, antennating the goby's tail while foraging just outside the burrow; the goby flicks its tail to signal danger and both retreat together. Cross-source notes: maximum size estimates range from 4 cm (reefapp, Wikipedia) to 7.6 cm / 3 in (LiveAquaria), with the written 4-7 cm range averaging across sources. Minimum tank size guidance ranges from ~50 L (reefapp) to 30 US gal / ~114 L (LiveAquaria's pistol-shrimp / goby article); the written 120 L value rounds to a nice common size and biases toward the larger figure because a pistol-goby pair benefits from a long, sand-rich footprint and a stable rockwork foundation. Salinity guidance ranges from SG 1.023-1.025 (LiveAquaria) to SG 1.026 (~35 ppt, reefapp and Aqua Imports); the written 1.023-1.026 range spans both. The cavitation-bubble snap is loud enough to be audible through tank glass and is generally harmless to tankmates, but the shrimp may rearrange substrate aggressively and occasionally undermine rockwork - always rest live rock directly on the glass rather than on the sand. Pistol shrimp are intolerant of copper-based medications and benefit from stable calcium, magnesium, iodine, and trace-element levels to support moulting. Generally reef-safe; isolated reports of taking very small ornamental shrimp or stunned fish exist, but coral and snail predation is not typical.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is rarely reported. Like most alpheids, females carry eggs under the abdomen and release planktonic larvae that are extremely difficult to rear without a dedicated low-flow larval system and appropriate microplankton-grade food. Most aquarium specimens are wild-collected from the Indo-West Pacific.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Tiger Snapping Shrimp (Symbiosis Shrimp) product page. Designated primary URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/ returned the catalog landing page; the working product page was located via search and used in its place. Also cross-referenced LiveAquaria's pistol-shrimp & gobies article (https://www.liveaquaria.com/article/201/) for symbiosis husbandry and 30-gallon minimum tank guidance.

additional.name: Aqua Imports
additional.notes: Designated secondary. Confirmed scientific name, Indo-Pacific origin, temperature 74-78 F (~23-26 C), pH 8.2-8.4, salinity 35 ppt (~SG 1.026), and sand-sifting / cavitation-snap behaviour.

additional.name: Wikipedia - Tiger pistol shrimp
additional.notes: Taxonomic and biological cross-check. Confirmed family Alpheidae, order Decapoda, Indo-West Pacific distribution, depth range 0-20 m, adult size 4-5 cm (body), goby partners (Cryptocentrus cinctus, Amblyeleotris guttata, Stonogobiops yasha, Amblyeleotris japonica), and cavitation-bubble snap mechanics.

additional.name: reefapp.net encyclopedia
additional.notes: Hobbyist care-guide cross-check. Confirmed peaceful temperament, reef-safe-with-caution rating relative to very small shrimp, ~50 L minimum recommendation, ~26 C target temperature, SG 1.026, and Indo-Pacific range.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally moults roughly once a month in adults; juveniles moult more frequently. Shed exoskeletons should be left in the tank as a calcium source. Stable salinity, calcium, magnesium, and trace-element / iodine levels support reliable moulting - sudden swings are commonly associated with failed moults. The shrimp is most vulnerable to predation in the day or two immediately after a moult while the new exoskeleton hardens.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Captive larval rearing of Alpheus bellulus is not well documented. Planktonic larvae are small and easily lost to filtration or sump entrainment; a dedicated low-flow larval-rearing setup would be required for any meaningful survival.
